author: @EgonSorensen
text:
Electric bikes are quite popular in Denmark - though they usually look like 'normal' bikes. Has removable batteries, which makes it easier to recharge.
Another thing I see more and more is electric tuk-tuk's, which is great when it rains (which it does quite a bit at times).
If I were to get a commute bike, I'd personally go after the Vespa type scooters, which usually has better brakes and is more sturdy. 
The Ado seems to have decent brakes, though no room for stuff (groceries, helmet, gloves, etc) on the back/under the seat.

author: @lamarrharding4776
text:
I have a 30+ year old "Iacoca" folding electric bicycle, after a year or so the batteries wouldn't hold as much of a charge and I  used it less and less. It's totally uncomfortable in freezing rain and hail is hell.

author: @nattsurfaren
text:
Robert, how would you design an e-bike so that you would absolutely fall in love with it and use it as your preferred way of transport as a 365 days cycle solution?
Maybe you could inspire some ideas for the e-bike manufacturers?
Some ideas that I have:
*Trike (protection from falling when you drive on ice and carry more batteries)
*Super long-range, two batteries (two because it is safe to explore and safe to come home)
*Supercharger support. (Hate waiting 3-4 hours when I recharge my batteries)
*Encapsulation (protection from rain)
*Switch from legal power to high power (cops will arrest you). Preferably hidden but easy to access.
*Really badass shock absorbers for smooth long-range driving.
I believe this would be irresistible for most people. 😃😃😃😃

replies.author: @hum2cme923
replies.text:
i like the way you think. this video & this comment brought an idea of what this would look like at scale & i thought well what if we had bike streets that had some sort of property via material or conductive paint/ink allowing them to absorb solar energy & transmit the energy to the bikes riding on them wirelessly. not sure how the latter would happen, but perhaps the wheels could connect to the battery & since one area on the perimeter of the wheel is always in contact with the path, you could get flow from the street to the bikes individual battery

also i think electric bikes at scale would make demand for larger group-transport systems for long distance more prominent in the world, so looking at making high speed trains, perhaps tunnels for such, & ferry boats that are efficient, low cost over long run, & ecologically holistic, & can move decent sized sums of people & their bikes from one location to another

thanks for the inspiration & guidance uncle robert!

replies.author: @nattsurfaren
replies.text: @hum2cme923 Imagine a road that could detect fossil fuel-based engine and automatically apply friction that would recharge a power bank. Then when an electric car comes it would take that energy and recharge its batteries.😆

author: @C-M-E
text:
When I was physically able to and lived in a city where such things weren't so spread out, I used to ride 27 miles a day for commuting and such (if you made people do their shopping and relegate them to a bicycle, you'd REALLY see efficient living!). I had a car but only used it for truly leisure purposes, which were rare but beloved when it did occur. I have one bike with over 60,000 miles that I've had and upgraded since I was in high school.   
Since moving to Texas in the early 2000s however, a vehicle is Mandatory. Everything is so spread out that normal commuting is 45-90 minutes just taking my kids on their loop to school. In the oil-loving area that built the fortunes of some, public transportation consists of a kite and your hopes/dreams. You can forget riding a bike; you'd be killed by the hordes of inattentive drivers who can't manage to separate their hands from their phones on a sunny day, let alone when the weather gets going. 
Anyway, while I'd love to see the IC engine continue with massive refinements to efficiency just because exhaust notes and the thrill of wide open throttle, electric cars have been on the wall for quite the while and Tesla's are quickly taking the place of all those Prius' that drive like road-raging rally racers on a public highway. I'm not one to dismiss some of the benefits of an electric drivetrain, but trading dino blood for coal in the gas vs electric debate isn't really a solution to me. There's TONS of clean methods of industrial scale electric production if people really want to do the work and get the ball rolling, though short of the massive wind farms cropping up all over, you don't see any of it being pursued. It's something I've researched and worked for the better part of half of my life and remain hopeful that one of the major breakthroughs will lead to a larger adoption rate (even better if I have a hand in it!).  I find it almost comical that ideas I had when I was just a wee boy are just now starting to circulate into feasibility, and undoubtedly had I the means back then to push them, I wonder what kind of world we'd have with efforts of people like myself, and especially you Robert, doing the heavy lifting.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
"I wonder what kind of world we'd have with efforts of people like myself,"

The same as you see now.
I interviewed for a position with an environmental lobbying company back in 05 and 06 and I suggested that the industry should first transition to hybrid vehicles where the gasoline engine is in fact a generator that recharges the batteries. I also suggested that environmental movements should use their position to negotiate a proper transition from petroleum to electric by not opposing the production of petroleum, but requiring that Oil companies invest a portion of their profits into building up the electric infrastructure.

The individual I was interviewing with told me that he cared about the environment more than I did and needless to say, I didn't get the job.

When people that claim they are for a cause reject a workable plan that will lead to success, and maintain a plan that not only has failed but will lead to failure, there is not much one can do to make a good change.

I made my hybrid idea known by email to the head of the Chevy Volt team when they were developing the Volt and that was the ultimate design of the volt.
It may be that they were thinking in those terms to begin with, but I was still pleasantly surprised.

It is sad that General Motors discontinued the volt for plug-in electrics as hybrids are the only real way to achieve long distance travel in an efficient manner as well as bridge the gap between a not ready infrastructure, and a ready infrastructure. 

One of the side benefits with the volt that I hadn't thought of; is that people could use their volt as a back up power supply because of it's on board generator.

replies.author: @carmichaelmoritz8662
replies.text: @scotttovey 🤔👍

replies.author: @carmichaelmoritz8662
replies.text: @scotttovey  i agree ,why not get the wealthy companies to invest into a greener tech.  i always wanted to buy a older model used hybrid and upgrade the battery pack to get more miles.  your gen back up idea is great due to the fact many people run into issues when leaving their portable gen sit to long not used. a vehicle is usually kept in good running order and ready to go , a perfect ready to go power backup gen system .  maybe the people that control us want things to stay as they are and thats why certain things are outed .

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@carmichaelmoritz8662 
"get the wealthy companies to invest into a greener tech"

It's not about getting wealthy companies to invest in greener tech because the so called green tech is not greener, it's dirtier. More environmentally harmful.

It's about getting profitable energy companies to diversify and invest in their future and America's future by investing a small percentage of their profits in research, finding and developing real, genuine, true to reality green energy that does not take away from the needs of the general population and does not produce more pollution than petroleum products.

If the so called green tech actually lived up to the hype, there would be no need for government subsidies or government mandates. Investors would invest in them without the need to be mandated.

"Environmentalist wackos" as Rush Limbaugh called them, are in fact wacko. They have absolutely no concern for people and demand mandated policies even if those policies will deprive people of heat in the winter time and kill them. The debacle in Texas during their freeze snap that actually killed people as a result, is proof of this. Did the green energy pushes take a step back ans suppose that the tech wasn't ready for prime time yet? No, they simply doubled down on their policy demand.

For the most part, the push for green energy tech is not about saving the planet or the environment. It's about destroying America's competitive edge in the world market. What the marxist pinheads don't realize is that Americans are not as passive, agreeable, and submissive as the other nations like Venezuela that they took over and brought down to economic poverty. 

American's were born to bring down and overthrow totalitarian depots. 
It's our national heritage, it's in our genetic code. No matter how long they have been falsely teaching and misinforming us, eventually the full blown truth of reality comes out and when it does; real Americans will stand up, and speak up, and when push comes to shove, kick the tyrants out of office and put them in prison where they belong, and kick all fascism, including marxism out of the land.
